results.possibleIdentity: Sheila Berkley (1945–2007) was an American sculptor and artist active during the late 1960s and 1970s, best known for her work in creating public art environments and children's playgrounds. She was a participant in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, and her work was featured in the E.A.T. exhibition 'Some More Beginnings' at the Brooklyn Museum (1968–1969), which showcased collaborations between artists and engineers. Berkley gained further recognition for her 'portable playground' installations, one of which was specifically included in the landmark 1977 exhibition 'Women in American Architecture: A Historic and Contemporary Perspective' at the Brooklyn Museum to make the gallery accessible to mothers and children. Her artistic practice often bridged the gap between functional design and social engagement, utilizing materials to create interactive public spaces.

results.possibleIdentity: Gary Stephan (born 1942) is a prominent American abstract artist, painter, and sculptor who was a significant figure in the New York art scene during the late 1960s and 1970s. After receiving his MFA from the San Francisco Art Institute in 1967, he returned to New York City and became a studio assistant to Jasper Johns (1968–1969). During this period, he was part of the influential artist community at 76 Jefferson Street alongside figures such as Neil Jenney and John Duff, where he experimented with non-traditional industrial materials like polyvinyl chloride. His early work was championed by the David Whitney Gallery and included in the Whitney Biennials of 1971 and 1973. While his primary practice is abstract painting and sculpture, he is historically associated with the experimental circles of the late 1960s that intersected with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), largely through his close professional ties to Jasper Johns and his engagement with the formal and technological questions of the era, which he later explored in writings for Artforum.

results.possibleIdentity: Anthony (Tony) Towne (1933–1980) was a poet, satirist, and writer active in New York City and Block Island during the late 1960s and 1970s. He was the long-time companion of the prominent lawyer and lay theologian William Stringfellow, with whom he shared a home in East Harlem and later on Block Island. Towne was deeply involved in the social and anti-war movements of the era; in August 1970, he and Stringfellow were famously indicted for "harboring a fugitive" after the anti-war activist Daniel Berrigan was apprehended by the FBI at their Block Island residence. As an artist, Towne's poetry and satirical essays appeared in publications such as The New Yorker and motive magazine. He is best known for his work "Excerpts from the Diaries of the Late God" (1968) and for co-authoring several books with Stringfellow, including "The Bishop Pike Affair" (1967), "Suspect Tenderness: The Ethics of the Berrigan Witness" (1971), and "The Death and Life of Bishop Pike" (1976). While an explicit membership in Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) is not documented in the primary search results, Towne operated within the same experimental, interdisciplinary, and socially engaged New York creative circles that characterized the E.A.T. organization during its peak years.
